content.assessment: From a public-finance lens, the status quo preserves current fiscal allocation patterns—identical per-pupil funding across selective and non-selective tracks despite documented divergence in outcomes and access (F-live-education_finance-06). Monitoring itself is low-cost but generates no reallocation of resources or change in incentive structures that currently reward private exam-preparation investment over school-level quality improvement. The opportunity cost is the continued fiscal subsidization of a system in which advantaged families capture returns disproportionately (F-live-education_finance-04), while affordability of the current arrangement is not itself in question—the fiscal magnitude of maintaining three parallel tracks remains modest in system-wide budget terms (F-live-education_finance-01) but its distributive incidence is regressive.
opportunities: Monitoring could generate the cost data currently missing, enabling future proposals to be fiscally evaluated with better evidence
strengths: No new fiscal outlay required beyond monitoring infrastructure, making this the most fiscally conservative option
Preserves existing budget certainty for maintainers and families, avoiding transition costs
threats: Continued divergence in value-added performance across tracks (F-live-education_finance-03) implies growing implicit inequity in returns to public spending, even as nominal per-pupil costs remain flat
Demographic pressure on comprehensive schools could raise the fiscal cost of inaction if closures accelerate the concentration of low-cost-to-serve advantaged students in surviving tracks
weaknesses: Perpetuates a funding model that provides identical per-pupil resources to tracks with divergent access and outcomes, without addressing the underlying incentive misalignment (F-live-education_finance-06)
Does nothing to alter the fiscal incentives that reward private-preparation investment over public quality investment
